stories.headline: Broncos, CDOT Finalize $45.8 Million Burnham Yard Purchase Agreement
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos and the Colorado Department of Transportation have finalized a formal agreement for the team to purchase the 58-acre Burnham Yard railyard site for $45.8 million, moving the franchise one step closer to building a new stadium. CDOT originally purchased the site in 2021 for $50 million, and the proceeds from the sale will cover the agency's obligations to lenders.

The Broncos first announced Burnham Yard as their preferred stadium site in September 2025. The team expects to close on the purchase this fall, with the target opening for the new stadium set for 2031.

The stadium and surrounding mixed-use development are slated to be funded by Broncos ownership, with the city and state providing support for public improvements and no new taxes. One remaining hurdle involves Denver Water, which will need to relocate facilities from the site; Denver Water officials have said they are committed to making Burnham Yard the stadium home as long as any impacted facilities are fully replaced at no expense to ratepayers.

stories.headline: Renck: Nuggets Face Long Road Back to Title Contention, Avalanche Outlook More Optimistic
stories.summary:
Denver Post columnist Terry Renck argues that the Colorado Avalanche's first-round sweep by the Vegas Golden Knights represents an aberration, while the Denver Nuggets' playoff failures reflect a deeper structural trend. Renck contends the Avs could return to Stanley Cup contention with a new coach and an improved power play, whereas the Nuggets face a far steeper climb given the current NBA landscape dominated by the Oklahoma City Thunder and San Antonio Spurs.

On the Nuggets' offseason, ESPN has reported the team will likely move either Cam Johnson or Christian Braun. Renck argues that Braun's five-year, $21.6 million-per-season extension has become a difficult contract to move given his declining performance, including a career-low 30.1 percent from three-point range last season and a backslid playoff showing.

Renck's recommended path for the Nuggets is to trade Johnson, whose expiring contract would attract contenders and return draft capital, then attempt to move Aaron Gordon to re-sign Peyton Watson. He suggests the team may not be better next season but could be better positioned for a championship window in 2029 during the final years of Nikola Jokic's contract.

stories.headline: Rockies Top Prospects Could See June Promotions as Team Struggles at 20-37
stories.summary:
With the Colorado Rockies sitting at 20-37 and mired in a five-game losing streak, attention is turning to the farm system for signs of hope. Colorado's No. 6 prospect Cole Carrigg has been a standout at Triple-A, batting .345 with a .938 OPS, 27 stolen bases, and strong extra-base production through 50 games, making him a candidate for promotion as early as June.

The Rockies' top-rated prospect has posted more modest numbers at Triple-A this season, batting .251/.384/.327 with 53 strikeouts in 45 games. Analysts suggest he is not yet ready for a promotion and could be a September call-up candidate at the earliest, given the organization's desire not to rush his development.

The Rockies remain 11 games behind the Los Angeles Dodgers in the NL West. The team's struggles have renewed focus on player development as the primary source of optimism heading into the summer months.

stories.headline: Denver Man Sentenced to 22 Years for Child Sex Trafficking in Larimer County
stories.summary:
A Larimer County District Court judge has sentenced Ivan Banuelos Gandara, 23, of Denver to 22 years in the Colorado Department of Corrections following his guilty plea to human trafficking and child sex exploitation charges. The sentencing was handed down this week, with prosecutors dismissing several additional charges including child prostitution, sex assault, enticement, internet luring and drug offenses as part of the plea agreement reached in March.

Police arrested Gandara in April 2024 after a 13-year-old girl reported he was forcing her to have sex in exchange for money, food and marijuana. Investigators discovered a second 13-year-old victim during the course of the investigation.

Eighth Judicial District Attorney Gordon McLaughlin praised the courage of the victims for coming forward and credited the multi-disciplinary human trafficking team that supported them through the case. McLaughlin said the partnerships between agencies are essential to handling the complexity of human trafficking prosecutions and ensuring victims are heard.

stories.headline: Judge Signals He Could Block Deportation of Boulder Firebomber's Family
stories.summary:
Senior U.S. District Judge John Kane indicated in federal court Friday that he is prepared to intervene if the executive branch moves to deport the family of Mohamed Soliman, the man who killed one woman and burned 13 others in a June 2025 antisemitic attack on Boulder's Pearl Street Mall. Kane stopped short of issuing a formal order but warned that he would entertain an immediate hearing if federal authorities attempted to arrest and remove Soliman's ex-wife and children.

Soliman's defense team has argued that his ex-wife and children are critical witnesses should prosecutors pursue the death penalty in the federal hate-crimes case, and that deporting them could violate Soliman's constitutional rights. Soliman pleaded guilty in state court this month and was sentenced to life in prison; he has offered to plead guilty in the federal case if prosecutors take the death penalty off the table.

Federal prosecutor Melissa Hindman said Friday that any deportation of the family would require at least 30 days' notice and that the countdown has not yet started, with the process potentially taking up to 90 days. Judge Kane set the case to return to court in late June for an update, noting the nation is 'closer to chaos in our system than we have ever been before.'

stories.headline: Colorado School Funding Reaches $10.2 Billion Despite $1.5 Billion Budget Gap
stories.summary:
Colorado lawmakers increased K-12 education spending to nearly $10.2 billion for the coming fiscal year despite being forced to close a roughly $1.5 billion state budget hole, according to the Colorado Sun. The General Assembly continued rolling out a new school funding formula adopted in 2024, with legislators largely shielding education from the deep cuts that affected other parts of the state budget.

Not all programs survived the session. The Teacher Recruitment Education and Preparation program, which helps high school students pursue teaching careers, was cut and will end in 2027 after serving one more class of seniors. Denver Public Schools Chief of Finance Chuck Carpenter called the outcome 'a pretty good result for K-12 as a whole' given the difficult economic environment.

Funding questions remain unresolved. Lawmakers referred a measure to the November ballot asking voters to forgo TABOR refunds to direct more money to education, and signature gathering is underway for a separate measure that would introduce a graduated income tax to replace Colorado's flat income tax structure. Approval of either or both measures could provide additional funding for the new school finance formula and protect districts from future budget pressure.

stories.headline: Pinnacol Assurance Ballot Measure Shelved After Business, Labor Groups Reach Legislative Deal
stories.summary:
A coalition of business and labor groups has reached an agreement to pursue a legislative overhaul of Pinnacol Assurance rather than take the issue to Colorado voters, according to the Denver Gazette. Colorado Succeeds, a business CEO coalition that had deposited more than $252,000 into an issue committee, has withdrawn its ballot measure in favor of a framework centered on legislation sponsored by Senate Majority Leader Robert Rodriguez, D-Denver.

The agreement involves Pinnacol, the state's largest workers' compensation insurer holding roughly half the market, the Colorado Building and Construction Trades Council, IBEW 111, and Colorado Succeeds. Under the framework, Pinnacol would become a special-purpose authority treated as a mutual insurance company, allowing it to sell insurance in other states — the primary goal Pinnacol has sought in separating from the state.

Key provisions include keeping Pinnacol as the insurer of last resort, retaining employees in the state pension plan, and expanding the board from nine to 11 members with a union representative added for the first time. The AFL-CIO had remained opposed to separation throughout the negotiations, but other labor groups moved forward without their participation.

stories.headline: Denver International Airport to End Security Line Reservation System After June 27
stories.summary:
Denver International Airport will discontinue its security line reservation system effective June 27, airport officials announced this week. The system, which allowed travelers to book a spot in the security queue for flights between 6 a.m. and 9 p.m., was introduced to manage congestion before the completion of major terminal improvements.

With the opening of two new 17-lane security checkpoints — the West checkpoint in 2024 and the East checkpoint in 2025 — average wait times have dropped to five minutes or less, making the reservation system 'no longer necessary,' according to airport officials. Both checkpoints were part of a $2.1 billion terminal renovation at DIA.

Travelers with existing reservations will be able to honor them, and new reservations will continue to be accepted through the final day of June 27. After that date, all passengers will proceed through security without an appointment.

stories.headline: Red Robin Sells 30 Pacific Northwest Restaurants for $23.5 Million to Pay Down Debt
stories.summary:
Englewood-based Red Robin Gourmet Burgers has sold 30 of its Pacific Northwest restaurant locations to Evergreen Dining for $23.5 million as part of the chain's ongoing turnaround plan. The proceeds will be used to pay down debt as the company works to stabilize its finances.

Red Robin, which is headquartered in the Denver suburb of Englewood, has been executing a restructuring strategy that includes divesting underperforming or non-core locations. The sale of the Pacific Northwest restaurants represents a significant reduction in the chain's company-owned footprint.

The burger chain has faced sustained financial pressure in recent years as casual dining competition intensifies. The Evergreen Dining transaction is among the more concrete steps the company has taken to shore up its balance sheet.

stories.headline: Denver to Host 12,000 Participants for Transplant Games of America in June
stories.summary:
Denver will serve as the host city for the 2026 Transplant Games of America, scheduled for June 18–23, with roughly 12,000 participants expected from across the country. Organized by the nonprofit Transplant Life Foundation, the six-day event brings together transplant recipients, living donors, donor families, future recipients and healthcare professionals for athletic competition and educational programming.

The event features more than 20 competitive events ranging from swimming, cycling and running to darts, cornhole and trivia, along with more than 60 special programs. This year will mark the first appearance of a team composed entirely of military veterans connected to transplantation. Forty-three state teams will compete for gold, silver and bronze medals.

Mark McIntosh, chair of the Denver host committee and himself a kidney transplant recipient following a rare cancer diagnosis, said the Games are designed to raise awareness about the ongoing need for organ donation. He noted that 550,000 Americans are currently on dialysis, with that number projected to reach one million within a decade.

stories.headline: Harry Potter Broadway Play, Exhibition and Drone Show Headline Denver's Magical Summer
stories.summary:
Denver is set for an unusually Harry Potter-heavy summer, beginning with the Tony Award-winning stage play 'Harry Potter and the Cursed Child' at the Buell Theatre from May 30 through June 21, with tickets starting at $58. The play, billed as the eighth story in the series, follows Harry Potter's son Albus and debuts in Denver a decade after it premiered in London's West End.

Starting June 26, 'Harry Potter: The Exhibition' will arrive along South Broadway, featuring authentic props and original costumes from the films, interactive activities including potion brewing and spell casting, and food items such as Chocolate Frogs and Butterbeer. Tickets start at $28.

Additional events include a 1,200-drone aerial show at Auraria Campus on July 10–11 and an appearance by actress Bonnie Wright, who portrayed Ginny Weasley, at Fan Expo Denver this weekend. The surge of Potter programming coincides with the 25th anniversary of the first film and anticipation for HBO Max's upcoming television adaptation of the series, set to premiere Christmas Day.

stories.headline: Denver Water Drought Restrictions: How to Report Neighbors Wasting Water
stories.summary:
With Stage 1 drought restrictions in effect, Denver Water has set up an online form and phone line for residents to report water waste by neighbors or businesses. Under the current rules, outdoor watering is limited to twice per week, and residents can flag violations through the utility's website or by calling 303-893-2444; park water waste can be reported by dialing 311.

The Denver Board of Water Commissioners enacted the Stage 1 restrictions in March in response to Colorado's drought status, with a goal of reducing average customer water usage by 20 percent. Larger customers have also been assigned water budgets under the restrictions.

The utility's dual 17-lane security checkpoints — part of a broader $2.1 billion terminal renovation — have cut average wait times to five minutes or less, rendering the reservation system unnecessary, officials said. Residents are encouraged to use the reporting tools to help the utility enforce compliance as dry conditions persist across the metro area.

weather.notable: Nearly 9 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I love when they say a baseball pitcher has a 'live arm.' As opposed to what? A dead arm? 'He's pitching great, especially considering the arm is from a corpse.'

topStory.headline: Federal Judge Orders Trump's Name Removed From Kennedy Center, Blocks Planned Closure
topStory.summary:
A federal judge ruled Friday that President Donald Trump's name was illegally added to the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts in Washington, D.C., and ordered the administration to remove it from the building's façade and all official materials within two weeks. U.S. District Judge Christopher Cooper also blocked the administration's plan to close the nationally recognized cultural institution for major renovations that had been scheduled to begin in July and last approximately two years.

Judge Cooper, an Obama appointee, found that the Kennedy Center board's March 16 vote to close the facility was "ill-informed and seemingly preordained" and that the board had "overstepped its statutory bounds" by unilaterally adding Trump's name to the center. Cooper wrote that Congress established the Kennedy Center's name by statute, and only Congress holds the authority to change it, adding that no other individual may be memorialized on the building's front portico without congressional authorization.

President Trump responded sharply on his Truth Social platform, calling the judge's decision an overreach and saying the judge "should be ashamed of himself." Trump announced he was withdrawing from his proposed renovation effort and returning control of the institution to Congress, writing that without the freedom to lead the project on his own terms, he had "no interest in continuing what could only be a hopeless journey."

The ruling represents the latest legal setback for Trump's efforts to reshape landmarks and institutions in the nation's capital. The administration had positioned the Kennedy Center renovation as a signature cultural initiative, and the planned closure would have significantly disrupted the center's programming and operations for the duration of the work. It remains unclear whether Congress will take any action in response to the ruling or whether the administration will appeal the decision.
